title: Convert blocking sync image processing to streaming worker pool with backpressure
business_context: Photo upload endpoint resizes images using sharp synchronously, blocking the event loop for 200-800ms per request. Under load p99 latency reaches 12s and the event loop lag alarm fires constantly. Need non-blocking processing with bounded concurrency and backpressure.
constraints: Cannot increase memory footprint significantly
Must handle 100 concurrent uploads
Failed jobs must retry with exponential backoff
Response must still return the resized URL on success

before_state.description: Inline sharp call in request handler.

issues: Sharp processing happens on main thread - blocks event loop
No concurrency cap - 100 simultaneous uploads spawn 100 sharp pipelines

reasoning: Sharp uses libvips which has its own threads, but the await boundaries still serialize allocation work on the main thread. Better: use piscina worker pool with a bounded queue. Workers do the resize off-thread. Backpressure: when queue length exceeds threshold, return 503 with Retry-After header. Frontend retries. For long-running jobs, switch to async pattern: enqueue, return job id, client polls or subscribes via socket. Tradeoff vs Worker Threads directly: piscina handles pool management, queueing, and abort signals - 50 lines saved. Tradeoff vs separate microservice: overkill for image resize; in-process worker pool is enough.

after_state.description: piscina worker pool with bounded queue, async job pattern, retry with backoff.

files.purpose: Pure worker function - off-main-thread image processing
Pool with bounded queue and backpressure via 503; async writes
Controller delegates to pool-backed service; sets Retry-After on backpressure
Client respects Retry-After with exponential backoff on 503

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add piscina dep and worker file; service still calls sharp inline
Step 2: Switch service to pool; deploy with maxThreads=2 to verify
Step 3: Ramp maxThreads to cpus-1; monitor event-loop-lag metric
Step 4: Add 503/Retry-After path; ship frontend retry logic
Step 5: Remove old sync code after 30 days
testing_strategy: Load test: 100 RPS for 2 min, assert event loop lag p99 < 50ms (was 800ms). Backpressure test: oversaturate, assert 503s come back with Retry-After. Worker crash test: throw inside worker, assert pool replaces it and subsequent jobs succeed. Memory test: 10k uploads, assert no leak.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would just await sharp - already 'async' in their head - but sharp's heavy decoding still blocks via libuv synchronous calls. Or they'd spawn child_process per image - 50ms fork overhead and unbounded process count.

title: Build notification center with read state sync across tabs and devices via Socket.IO
business_context: Users complain that closing a notification on web doesn't update mobile and vice versa. Need server-authoritative unread state with realtime sync. Must handle a user with 5 active devices.
constraints: Server is source of truth - clients reconcile
Must not race when user clicks multiple notifications quickly
Offline reads must sync on reconnect
Cannot lose notifications during transitions

before_state.description: Per-device local unread set in localStorage. No server sync.

issues: Read state local to one device
Manual JSON serialization, race on multi-click

reasoning: Model: NotificationRead { userId, notificationId, readAt } - presence indicates read. Endpoint POST /notifications/:id/read is idempotent (upsert). On success, broadcast to user's room. Socket.IO namespace /notifications joins room user:${id} on connect. Mark-read POST + broadcast keeps all devices in sync. Offline: on reconnect, GET /read-since=last_sync timestamp returns all reads since then to backfill. Tradeoff vs storing read=true on Notification doc: makes it harder to add 'first read at' analytics; separate read records are cleaner.

after_state.description: Server-authoritative read state. Idempotent endpoint. Socket broadcast to user room. Offline reconciliation on reconnect.

files.purpose: Idempotent mark-read with broadcast only on first write; since endpoint for offline catchup
Simple namespace - user joins their own room on connect
Hook handles initial load, socket sync, optimistic UI, offline catchup on reconnect
Simple consumer of hook - state lives in hook

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add NotificationRead model and endpoints alongside old behavior
Step 2: Frontend migrates to new hook; localStorage read state becomes secondary
Step 3: Backfill: on first mark-read call per user, also import localStorage read ids as a one-time merge
Step 4: Remove localStorage code after 30 days
testing_strategy: Multi-device test: mark read on device A, assert device B sees it within 1s. Offline test: disconnect device B, mark read on A, reconnect B, assert sync via since-endpoint. Race test: click same notification twice rapidly, assert single broadcast and idempotent DB state. Hammer test: 50 marks/sec from one user, assert no duplicate reads.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would store read state in Notification doc as a boolean. Two devices marking concurrently both pass the if-already-read check; broadcast fires twice. Idempotent upsert+unique-index combo is the structural fix.

title: Replace ad-hoc error throwing with typed error hierarchy and centralized error middleware
business_context: Errors are thrown as plain strings or generic Error across the codebase. Status codes and response shapes vary per route. Frontend struggles to render meaningful messages. Need a coherent error taxonomy with consistent HTTP mapping and stable error codes for client logic.
constraints: Cannot break existing client error parsing
Stack traces must not leak to production responses
Errors must be logged with severity and request context
Migration should be incremental

before_state.description: Random string errors, inconsistent status codes, throw Error inline.

issues: Three different error envelope shapes in one function
Strings as error codes - cannot programmatically branch on client
No correlation between server logs and client errors
Leaks raw error.message which may include stack info

reasoning: Typed error hierarchy with AppError base. Each subclass has code (machine identifier), status (HTTP), severity (log level). Centralized middleware maps to response. Services throw domain errors with semantic names; middleware translates to HTTP. Response shape: { error: { code, message, requestId } }. Frontend gets stable codes to switch on. Migration: introduce the hierarchy, write new controllers with it, gradually convert old throws. Tradeoff vs http-errors lib: simpler but doesn't enforce code/severity. Tradeoff vs Zod-typed errors with runtime decoding: overkill for server-throws.

after_state.description: Typed AppError hierarchy. Central middleware maps to consistent shape. Stable error codes. Structured logging.

files.purpose: Base AppError with code, status, severity; common subclasses encode HTTP semantics
Wraps unknown errors as InternalError; logs at appropriate severity; production hides 5xx messages and stacks
Uses typed errors - status, code, severity all derived from class
Client mirror - typed error with stable code property for branching
Client switches on stable code - decouples message from logic

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add AppError hierarchy and updated middleware; legacy routes still return ad-hoc shapes
Step 2: Migrate one controller at a time to throw typed errors
Step 3: Migrate frontend to use ApiError parsing
Step 4: Add ESLint rule banning throw new Error('...') in controllers/services
Step 5: Audit logs for stable codes; build dashboards counting by code
testing_strategy: Snapshot test: known error scenarios produce expected { code, message, requestId, status }. Severity test: simulate each subclass, assert logger called with correct level. Production-mode test: NODE_ENV=production, assert 500 responses redact message and stack.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would standardize the envelope but keep status hardcoded per route, so adding a new error needs touching the route. Or they'd return error.message verbatim in prod, leaking stack info. Or they'd skip the client mirror, forcing brittle message-string parsing.

title: Plug XSS hole in markdown comment renderer; introduce sanitized renderer and Content-Security-Policy
business_context: User-submitted markdown comments are rendered with dangerouslySetInnerHTML after marked parsing. A bug bounty showed that bypasses marked's sanitizer. Need defense-in-depth: server sanitization, client allowlist, CSP.
constraints: Existing comments stored as raw markdown must continue rendering correctly
Code blocks must keep syntax highlighting
Links must open in new tab with rel=noopener
Cannot break inline images from approved CDNs

before_state.description: Raw markdown rendered with marked, no sanitizer.

issues: No sanitization - XSS via crafted HTML inside markdown
No CSP fallback if sanitizer is bypassed
Stores arbitrary markdown without server-side sanity check on size or content

reasoning: Layered defense: 1) Server-side validation enforces size and rejects obvious script tags before storage. 2) Client renders via marked + DOMPurify allowlist. 3) CSP header on app responses prevents inline scripts from executing even if sanitizer is bypassed. 4) Links rewritten to rel=noopener target=_blank. 5) Images restricted to approved hosts via DOMPurify hooks. Tradeoff vs server-rendered HTML: caching, but markdown rendering is fast on client and avoids stale-render bugs. Tradeoff vs disabling raw HTML in markdown entirely: breaks intentional <details> etc.; allowlist gives both.

after_state.description: Server validation, DOMPurify allowlist on client, CSP header, link/image hardening.

files.purpose: CSP header restricts inline scripts and limits image origins
Length cap and obvious-XSS pattern rejection before storage
DOMPurify with tag/attr allowlist; link/image hardening hooks

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add CSP in report-only mode to surface violations from legitimate content
Step 2: Switch CSP to enforce after one week
Step 3: Ship server validation; backfill scan existing comments for forbidden patterns and mark suspicious for review
Step 4: Roll out DOMPurify rendering
Step 5: Penetration retest
testing_strategy: XSS regression suite of 30 payloads from OWASP cheat sheet - assert all neutralized. CSP test: inject an inline <script> via DOM, assert browser blocks. Image allowlist test: post markdown with image from disallowed host, assert image removed. Link test: post markdown link, assert rendered with rel=noopener.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would set sanitize: true in marked options - marked deprecated this in 4.x and it never was a strong sanitizer. They'd add CSP without testing, blocking legitimate CDN images. They'd sanitize on render only and skip server validation, leaving stored XSS payloads sitting in DB waiting for a renderer bug.

title: Replace useState-per-filter dashboard with URL-synced filter store enabling shareable links
business_context: Analytics dashboard has 12 filter controls held in useState. Sales team wants to share filtered views via URL. Current state lives only in component memory; reloading loses filters. Need URL as source of truth with type-safe parsing.
constraints: Filter URLs must remain backward compatible if we ever add/remove a filter
Must work with React Router v7
No new top-level deps > 10KB
Must support deep linking from emails

before_state.description: Each filter is a useState; nothing in URL.

issues: No way to share a filtered view
Filter type drift between components reading them

reasoning: Build a useFilters hook that reads/writes URL search params via React Router's useSearchParams, validated against a Zod schema. Schema provides defaults and types. Updates push or replace history entries depending on context (search updates use replace to avoid back-button spam; navigations push). Tradeoff vs jotai/atomWithLocation: works but adds a dep. Tradeoff vs raw query strings without schema: type-unsafe, leaks invalid filters. Zod ensures runtime correctness.

after_state.description: URL is source of truth. Zod-validated filter shape. Shareable links. History strategy chosen per action.

files.purpose: Single source of truth for filter shape with defaults
Hook reads/writes URL params; defaults applied; replace by default to avoid history spam
Filters via hook; URL reflects state; share link is just the current URL

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add schema + hook; existing useState-based dashboard untouched
Step 2: Convert one filter at a time to hook; verify URL reflects
Step 3: Convert all; remove useState filter declarations
Step 4: Add link copy button
testing_strategy: URL parse test: visit ?segment=paid&from=2026-03-01 directly, assert filters set. Invalid URL test: ?segment=bad falls back to default. Reset test: clears URL. History test: changing segment uses replace (no new history entry).
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would JSON-stringify the filter object into one query param - breaks shareable links and bookmarks. They'd not validate, letting invalid filters reach the API. They'd push on every change, polluting history.

title: Add scrollytelling 3D hero with GSAP-driven camera and dispose discipline
business_context: Marketing wants a scroll-driven 3D hero on the homepage that morphs through 4 keyframe scenes as the user scrolls. Must not regress LCP, must work on low-end mobile, must clean up perfectly when the user navigates away.
constraints: LCP must remain < 2.5s on 4G
Bundle for hero < 200KB gzipped
Reduced motion preference disables scrolling animation
No layout shift on hero load

before_state.description: Static hero PNG.

reasoning: Code-split the 3D hero with React.lazy + Suspense. Boot a lightweight placeholder image first to keep LCP. Hydrate the canvas after first paint. GSAP ScrollTrigger drives a timeline. Camera moves along a Catmull-Rom spline computed from keyframes. Reduced motion: skip ScrollTrigger, snap to final state. Dispose: useEffect cleanup walks scene.traverse and disposes geometries/materials; r3f handles WebGLRenderer cleanup. Tradeoff vs Lottie: 2D only - no depth. Tradeoff vs CSS-only scroll: limited expression and no 3D parallax.

after_state.description: Lazy-loaded 3D hero with GSAP-driven camera spline, reduced-motion fallback, full dispose discipline.

files.purpose: GSAP-driven camera spline with reduced-motion fallback; canvas lazy via parent
Code-split hero - placeholder image renders first for LCP

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add Hero3D and lazy import; keep PNG hero as the Suspense fallback
Step 2: Measure LCP on production - assert no regression
Step 3: Iterate keyframes with marketing
Step 4: Ship reduced motion testing
testing_strategy: Lighthouse audit on home page - LCP < 2.5s mobile slow 4G. Reduced motion test: matchMedia.matches=true, assert no ScrollTrigger created and final state visible. Dispose test: navigate away, assert WebGLRenderer.info.memory.geometries returns to 0.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would import Hero3D directly - blocks initial bundle and tanks LCP. They'd skip reduced-motion handling - accessibility regression. They'd forget ScrollTrigger.kill() and pin handlers leak across navigation.

title: Add per-tenant rate limit and quota enforcement with billing-aware tiers
business_context: After multi-tenancy ship, free-tier orgs hammer the API and degrade enterprise tier. Need quota per org per endpoint category, with tier-based limits, accurate counting across instances, and admin overrides.
constraints: Limits must reset at tenant-local midnight
Enterprise tier must be unblockable
Cannot impact request latency by more than 5ms p99
Quota state must survive Redis restarts

before_state.description: Single global limiter per IP; no notion of tenant or tier.

reasoning: Quota strategy: per (orgId, category) counter in Redis with daily TTL keyed by tenant timezone date. Endpoint categories declared per-route. Tier config in Org doc (free=1k/day reads, pro=50k, enterprise=unlimited). Middleware fetches counter, increments, compares to tier limit. If over: 429 with X-RateLimit-Remaining headers. Counts piped to a separate analytics topic for billing. Latency: Redis INCR is ~0.5ms; pipeline with EXPIRE makes it one round trip. Tradeoff vs sliding window: daily quotas need clean midnight reset, fixed window is correct semantically.

after_state.description: Per-org per-category quota with tier-based limits; daily reset; bypass for enterprise; rate-limit headers.

files.purpose: Tenant-timezone daily quota with tier-based limits and standard rate-limit headers
Routes declare category - middleware enforces quota per category and tier
In-app banner warns when nearing limit; reads from a usage endpoint backed by same counters

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add Tier field to Org with default 'free' (but no enforcement)
Step 2: Add quota middleware in dry-run mode logging would-block events
Step 3: Mark enterprise orgs after billing reconciliation
Step 4: Enable enforcement; communicate to free-tier users
Step 5: Ship usage banner in frontend
testing_strategy: Hot-path latency: instrument middleware p99, assert < 5ms. Reset test: simulate midnight in tenant tz, assert key rotation. Tier bypass test: enterprise tier never gets 429. Concurrent counter test: 100 parallel requests, assert counter increments by exactly 100.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would use a fixed UTC midnight reset - confuses customers in APAC who get reset mid-business-day. They'd count in app memory - races across instances and lost counts on deploy. They'd not exempt enterprise, breaking the most important customers when limits configured wrong.

title: Add versioned API with v1/v2 routers, response transformer, and deprecation headers
business_context: Client teams need to evolve response shapes (rename fields, restructure nesting) but mobile v1 must keep working. Need URL-versioned API with shared service layer and per-version response transformers. Plus a deprecation-warning workflow so v1 can sunset.
constraints: Service and DAL must not be duplicated per version
Versions must coexist without if/else in controllers
Deprecation headers must inform clients
CI must fail if a v1 contract changes accidentally

before_state.description: Single unversioned routes. Response shape changes break clients.

issues: No versioning - any change breaks all clients
Service shape leaks directly to clients

reasoning: Mount versioned routers under /api/v1 and /api/v2. Shared controller calls service for domain object; per-version transformer maps to wire format. Deprecation: v1 middleware sets Deprecation and Sunset headers (RFC 8594). CI: contract tests against fixtures for each version - any drift fails. Tradeoff vs header-based versioning (Accept: application/vnd.app.v2+json): standard but harder for browsers and curl debugging. URL versioning wins for API ergonomics. Tradeoff vs evolutionary single-version: works for additive changes, breaks for renames.

after_state.description: v1 and v2 routers share service. Transformers per version. Deprecation headers on v1. Contract test fixtures pinned.

files.purpose: v1 wire shape (legacy dollars-as-number, flat customer id)
v2 wire shape (cents + currency, nested customer)
v1 router shares service; transformer maps to legacy shape; deprecation headers signal sunset
v2 router shares same service; transformer maps to new shape
Versioned mounts isolate breaking changes
Pinned contract fixture - CI compares to this on every PR

migration_strategy: Step 1: Refactor controller to return domain object; introduce transformer for v1 (no behavior change)
Step 2: Mount /api/v1 and /api/v2 routers; v1 keeps legacy shape, v2 introduces new shape
Step 3: Update clients incrementally - mobile v2 ships, web v2 ships
Step 4: Monitor v1 traffic per client; reach out to laggards
Step 5: After Sunset date, return 410 Gone on v1 endpoints
testing_strategy: Contract test runs against fixture: if v1 response shape changes by a single field, test fails. Header test: v1 response contains Deprecation and Sunset. Migration test: same underlying order returns differently shaped responses via v1 vs v2 endpoints.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would branch inside controllers (if version === 'v1' ...) - duplicates business logic in transformer ifs. They'd skip Sunset headers, leaving no way to inform clients. They'd rebuild service per version, doubling maintenance.

title: Eliminate query waterfall on order detail page with batched DataLoader-style coalescing
business_context: Order detail page renders 12 LineItem rows; each LineItem fetches its product via separate request. Page makes 13 sequential requests, taking 1.8s. Need to batch product fetches without rewriting LineItem to know about siblings.
constraints: LineItem component must not need to know about other LineItems
Must not introduce GraphQL
Cache must survive component unmount/remount during the same session
Memory must not grow unbounded

before_state.description: Each LineItem fetches product independently.

issues: N independent requests per page render

reasoning: Client side: batch loader collects productId calls during a short window (~5ms via microtask), then issues one POST /products/batch with ids[]. Each caller gets resolved with their product. Cache by id with WeakRef + size cap so memory stays bounded. Server: add /products/batch endpoint accepting ids[], returning products[]. Use $in query. Tradeoff vs GraphQL DataLoader: similar concept; for our 1-endpoint use, a 60-line client utility is enough. Tradeoff vs prefetching on parent: works but couples LineItem to parent.

after_state.description: Client batched loader; server batch endpoint; in-memory cache.

files.purpose: Batch endpoint with id-order preservation and null for missing
Microtask-batched loader with bounded LRU cache
LineItem unchanged in API; uses loader which batches transparently

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add batch endpoint server-side; old single-get still works
Step 2: Ship loader on client, replace direct fetches in LineItem
Step 3: Audit other components doing per-row fetches; convert to loader
Step 4: Keep single-get endpoint for direct deep links
testing_strategy: Batching test: render order with 12 LineItems, assert exactly one /products/batch call. Cache test: re-render same LineItem, no new request. Concurrency test: 100 rapid loadProduct calls with mix of duplicates, assert single network request and correct resolution.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would prefetch all products in the parent and prop-drill. Works but couples and breaks if LineItem is rendered elsewhere. Or they'd debounce with setTimeout(50ms) - adds artificial latency vs microtask.

title: Encrypt PII fields at rest with envelope encryption and KMS rotation
business_context: Compliance audit requires PII (SSN-equivalent national id, DOB) encrypted at field level, not just disk encryption. Need envelope encryption with KMS-rotated KEKs, ability to re-encrypt without downtime, and queryable hashing for lookups.
constraints: Must support equality search on encrypted national_id
Key rotation must not require downtime
Existing data must be migrated in background
Decryption errors must fail loudly
Audit log of every decryption

before_state.description: PII stored plaintext in Mongoose docs.

reasoning: Envelope encryption: a Data Encryption Key (DEK) per record encrypts the field. The DEK is itself encrypted with a Key Encryption Key (KEK) from KMS. Stored: { ct, iv, tag, encDek, kekVersion }. For search on national_id: store an HMAC blind-index alongside ciphertext, key derived from a separate index secret. Lookup by HMAC, decrypt only the matched row. Rotation: introduce new KEK version, run background re-encrypt of DEKs (cheap - one KMS call per record), leave ciphertext alone. Field-level encryption via Mongoose getters/setters or a wrapper repo. Tradeoff vs MongoDB CSFLE: tight integration but Atlas-only and adds infra complexity. Application-level envelope is portable.

after_state.description: Envelope-encrypted PII fields with blind index for search; KMS rotation; audit on decrypt.

files.purpose: Envelope encrypt/decrypt and blind index for equality lookup
Cipher subdocuments + blind index field; legacy nationalId removed after migration
Service encrypts on write, computes blind index for search, audits decryption
Background re-wraps DEKs with new KEK; ciphertext untouched

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add cipher subdocuments; new writes encrypt; reads fall back to plaintext field if cipher absent
Step 2: Backfill: stream existing records, encrypt nationalId, compute blind index
Step 3: Verify counts match; remove plaintext field
Step 4: Run KEK rotation worker monthly
Step 5: Penetration retest
testing_strategy: Round-trip test: encrypt, decrypt, assert equality. Search test: insert customer with nationalId=X, search via blindIndex, assert returned. Tamper test: modify ct bytes, assert decryption throws (GCM auth). Rotation test: rotate KEKs, assert decryption still works using stored kekVersion.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would use AES-CBC without auth tag - vulnerable to padding-oracle. They'd derive encryption key from a fixed env var without KMS - no rotation possible. They'd skip the blind index, forcing decryption of every row on search.

title: Refactor optimistic mutation hell into a typed mutation queue with rollback and offline replay
business_context: Kanban board lets users move cards. Optimistic UI written ad-hoc per action: local move + fetch + on-error revert via setTimeout. Conflicts when user moves multiple cards fast. Offline = lost updates. Need a unified mutation queue with proper rollback semantics.
constraints: UI must remain instant
Failed mutations must show clear error and revert local state
Offline mutations must replay on reconnect
Order of mutations must be preserved

before_state.description: Each component handles its own optimistic update and rollback.

reasoning: Mutation queue: each mutation has { id, type, payload, optimistic, rollback, commit }. UI calls enqueue(mutation). Queue applies optimistic immediately, then processes mutations FIFO calling commit (server). On success, drops the rollback. On failure, runs rollback in reverse order for all dependent mutations. Offline: queue persists pending to IndexedDB; on reconnect, drains. Tradeoff vs TanStack Query mutations: handles much of this but full opt-in is invasive. A purpose-built queue (~150 LOC) gives us exact semantics.

after_state.description: Typed mutation queue with optimistic/rollback/commit + IndexedDB persistence + replay on reconnect.

files.purpose: Typed mutation queue with optimistic apply, FIFO commit, cascading rollback on failure
Kanban-specific store using the queue + window online listener for replay
Card now stateless - reads from store, dispatches mutation

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add MutationQueue lib alongside existing per-component logic
Step 2: Migrate Card move action to queue; keep other actions inline
Step 3: Migrate other actions
Step 4: Add IndexedDB persistence for offline
Step 5: Remove ad-hoc rollback code
testing_strategy: Concurrency test: dispatch 5 moves rapidly, assert order preserved in commits. Failure cascade: fail middle commit, assert all subsequent rolled back. Offline test: dispatch while offline, reconnect, assert drained.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would handle rollback per component with try/catch. Cascading dependencies between mutations are lost - move A then move A again on top: if first fails the second's rollback restores the wrong column.

title: Add live leaderboard with sorted-set scoreboard and snapshot+delta socket protocol
business_context: Gaming app needs a live leaderboard for 200k concurrent players with sub-second updates as scores change. Sending full leaderboard every change saturates network. Need snapshot+delta protocol with bounded payload.
constraints: Initial load must show top-100 in < 300ms
Updates per second per client must not exceed 2 messages
Player can see own rank even if outside top-100
Reconnect must reconcile correctly

before_state.description: Polling /leaderboard every 5s; full top-100 returned every time.

issues: Full collection scan + sort per request

reasoning: Use Redis sorted set ZADD/ZRANGE for O(log N) ranking. On score change, ZADD updates and we broadcast a delta to subscribers. Snapshot+delta protocol: on socket join, send full top-100 + caller's rank. Then send incremental deltas (entry moved, new entry, removal) at most 2/sec via debouncing. Reconnect: client tells server last applied delta version; server either resumes from version or sends fresh snapshot if version too old.

after_state.description: Redis sorted set scoreboard; snapshot+delta protocol with version; per-client rate limit.

files.purpose: Sorted-set scoreboard with O(log N) writes and O(log N + k) range reads
Snapshot+delta protocol with version-based resume; recent ring buffer for catchup
Hook tracks version; reconnects with since=version; merges deltas locally

migration_strategy: Step 1: Backfill Redis sorted set from existing ScoreModel data
Step 2: Update score-write path to also call leaderboardService.addScore and emit message bus
Step 3: Add socket namespace; ship behind flag
Step 4: Frontend uses new hook; old polling removed
Step 5: Decommission polling endpoint after 30 days
testing_strategy: Snapshot test: connect fresh client, assert receives top-100 in correct order. Delta test: write score that moves player from rank 50 to 30, assert delta sent to subscribers and top reflects. Reconnect test: drop connection, write 10 scores, reconnect with last version, assert client catches up via deltas not snapshot.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would send full top-100 on every score change. 200k clients * top-100 payload * 10 updates/sec = network meltdown. Or they'd skip the version mechanism, forcing full snapshot on every reconnect.

title: Move polling-based background jobs into BullMQ with reliable retries and dead-letter queue
business_context: Background work (send email, generate report, sync CRM) currently runs via setInterval in the API process. Failures lost, retries non-existent, mixing API and worker concerns causes deploy churn. Need proper queue.
constraints: Cannot lose in-flight work during cutover
Failed jobs must surface to operators
Workers must scale horizontally
Each job type must have a configurable concurrency

before_state.description: setInterval polling a 'jobs' collection in the API process.

issues: Job lock not atomic - races between instances
Mixed with API process - deploy of one ships the other

reasoning: BullMQ with Redis. Define a queue per job type for separate concurrency tuning. Workers run in dedicated process (Procfile separation). Retries with backoff configured per type. Failed jobs after max attempts land in a DLQ for ops review. API process only enqueues. Tradeoff vs Kafka: too heavy for this scale. Tradeoff vs SQS: cloud-locked.

after_state.description: BullMQ queues per type; dedicated worker process; retries + DLQ; API process only enqueues.

files.purpose: Queues + worker factories with per-type retries and concurrency
Typed enqueue facade used by API controllers
Dedicated worker entry point; graceful shutdown on SIGTERM
API now enqueues and returns job id; processing decoupled
Operator endpoints to inspect and retry failed jobs

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add BullMQ alongside JobModel poller; new code uses enqueue, old still polls
Step 2: Stand up worker process in deployment
Step 3: Drain JobModel pending jobs by enqueueing them; remove poller
Step 4: Add operator UI for failed queue
Step 5: Remove JobModel collection after 30 days
testing_strategy: Retry test: throw in processor 3 times, assert 4th attempt runs with exponential backoff. DLQ test: throw on all attempts, assert job lands in failed list with reason. Concurrency test: enqueue 20 jobs, assert max 10 process simultaneously. Graceful shutdown test: send SIGTERM mid-job, assert job completes before exit.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would keep workers inside the API process - one slow report blocks request handling. Or skip jobId uniqueness, allowing accidental duplicate emails on enqueue retries. Or skip backoff, hammering downstream services on failure.

title: Add product 3D viewer with GLB streaming, KTX2 textures, and aggressive caching
business_context: E-commerce wants 3D product views on detail pages. Models average 6MB GLB with 4MB textures. Need fast first paint, progressive loading, and cache reuse across product pages.
constraints: Initial TTFB < 1s
Total payload < 3MB before user interaction
Must work offline once cached
Must dispose properly when navigating away

before_state.description: Product detail page shows a flat image carousel.

reasoning: Use draco-compressed GLB + KTX2 textures (~70% smaller than PNG). r3f with Suspense and useGLTF for lazy loading. Service worker caches GLB by URL with Cache-First strategy. Show low-res placeholder image while model loads. Dispose: r3f handles geometry/material from useGLTF; clear cache on unmount via useGLTF.preload + clear.

after_state.description: Draco+KTX2 GLB streaming with service worker cache; placeholder image; dispose on unmount.

files.purpose: r3f canvas with draco+ktx2 GLB and Suspense; placeholder image fades out on load
Service worker caches GLB requests for offline reuse
Lazy 3D viewer with image fallback; first paint from placeholder image

migration_strategy: Step 1: Convert one product's images to GLB; add modelUrl field
Step 2: Ship lazy viewer; legacy products without modelUrl keep image
Step 3: Ship service worker for GLB caching
Step 4: Backfill GLBs for top-100 SKUs
Step 5: Monitor LCP regression
testing_strategy: Network test: hard reload product page, assert GLB downloaded once, second visit served from cache. Disposal test: navigate to/from product 100 times, assert WebGL contexts cleaned up. Reduced bandwidth: throttle to slow 3G, assert placeholder image visible immediately and model loads in background.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would inline-import 3D viewer - blocks initial bundle. They'd use uncompressed GLB - 25MB per model. They'd skip dispose - 100 navigations leak GPU memory.

title: Add audit trail with diff-based history page and per-field redaction
business_context: Compliance requires before/after audit on every change to sensitive entities. Engineers currently write ad-hoc audit log calls scattered through services - inconsistent and easy to forget. Need a centralized mechanism.
constraints: Must not double the write latency
Sensitive fields must be redacted in the audit log
History page must show field-by-field diffs
Must work even when changes bypass service layer (rare admin operations)

before_state.description: Random console.log or AuditLog.create calls. No diff. No redaction.

reasoning: Mongoose plugin captures pre/post document state on save/update, produces a diff, and writes audit entry. Sensitive fields configured per model are redacted to '[REDACTED]'. Plugin runs at model layer so even admin/CLI mutations get logged. History page renders diffs grouped by entity. Tradeoff vs middleware on service layer: plugin is more comprehensive. Tradeoff vs change streams: complex coupling between ops and stream events.

after_state.description: Mongoose plugin auto-emits audit on changes; sensitive fields redacted; history endpoint and UI.

files.purpose: Plugin captures snapshot before save and writes redacted diff after
User model registers audit plugin with passwordHash redacted
History endpoint per entity
History page renders before/after per field

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add plugin and apply to one model (User); legacy audit calls coexist
Step 2: Verify audit entries match prior expectations
Step 3: Apply plugin to other sensitive models (Order, Customer)
Step 4: Remove ad-hoc AuditLog.create calls
Step 5: Add UI link from entity detail to history view
testing_strategy: Unit: change one field, assert diff captures only that field. Redaction test: change passwordHash, assert audit entry shows '[REDACTED]'. Bulk-update test: model.updateOne({}, ...) - plugin should also capture (use schema.pre on updateOne). Performance test: measure save latency before/after, assert < 10% regression.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would log inside controller try-blocks. Misses CLI scripts, admin tools, migration scripts. They'd serialize the whole doc including passwordHash, leaking secrets into the audit collection.

title: Split per-tenant data into dedicated MongoDB databases with connection pool router
business_context: Largest enterprise customer requires data isolation at database level (not just row-level). Need a connection router that picks the right Mongo DB per tenant. Existing shared-DB customers must continue working unchanged.
constraints: Cannot perform downtime migration
Operational simplicity - few DBs not hundreds
Mongoose model definitions must be reused, not duplicated
Per-tenant connection pools must be bounded

before_state.description: Single Mongoose connection shared by all tenants.

issues: One DB for all tenants - cannot offer dedicated DB tier
Bound to default connection - can't redirect by tenant

reasoning: Router caches connections by tenant tier. Shared tier returns the default connection. Dedicated tier maps tenant -> connection by spinning up (or reusing) a connection to that tenant's DB cluster. Models are registered per-connection via reusable schema definitions. Service layer accepts a 'db' object (collection of models) returned by router. Tradeoff vs separate process per tenant: ops nightmare. Tradeoff vs shared cluster with separate DBs in same instance: works for our scale.

after_state.description: Connection router with bounded pool; per-tenant model registration; service layer uses router-provided models.

files.purpose: Connection router with shared/dedicated tiers and bounded pool
Schema definitions decoupled from connection - reusable per-tenant
Middleware resolves tenant DB and attaches to request
Controllers use models from request - automatically tenant-scoped

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add router and schema-def files; shared tier returns existing connection (no change)
Step 2: Migrate controllers to use req.tenantDb
Step 3: Provision dedicated DB for first enterprise customer; cutover with read-only window for data copy
Step 4: Document tier upgrade process
Step 5: Monitor pool eviction stats
testing_strategy: Isolation test: write order in tenant A's dedicated DB, query tenant B - assert nothing returned. Pool test: configure max 3, request 4 different dedicated tenants, assert eviction with cleanup. Cutover test: dry-run copy + read-only window in staging.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would create a new Mongoose connection on every request - exhausts file descriptors. They'd register models without checking exists, causing 'OverwriteModelError'. They'd skip eviction, leaking connections.

title: Eliminate React re-render storm in chart-heavy dashboard via memoization audit
business_context: Sales dashboard with 14 charts takes 800ms to interact after typing in a filter. Profiling reveals every keystroke re-renders all charts. Need disciplined memoization and stable references throughout.
constraints: Cannot rewrite charts from scratch
Bundle size cannot grow
Visual output must be byte-identical after refactor
Cannot adopt new state library

before_state.description: Inline functions in props, no memoization, derived data computed on every render.

issues: onPointClick is a new function each render - defeats React.memo on LineChart
options object is a new ref each render
Charts re-render whenever input changes even though data didn't change

reasoning: Three-prong fix: useMemo for derived data, useCallback for handlers, useMemo for options object. React.memo on chart components. The trick: ensure deps lists are stable - referential equality matters more than recomputation cost. Tradeoff vs Recoil/Jotai: introduces deps. Tradeoff vs useEvent: experimental. Standard memoization works.

after_state.description: Memoized derivation, stable handler/options refs, React.memo charts.

files.purpose: Memoized derivations + stable callbacks + module-scope options
Chart wrapped in React.memo - re-renders only when props referentially change
Dev tool for spotting unnecessary re-renders - useful in code review

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add React.memo to leaf chart components first
Step 2: Audit each parent for unstable refs; add useCallback/useMemo
Step 3: Move static config objects to module scope
Step 4: Add why-did-you-render diagnostics in dev
Step 5: Add ESLint react-hooks/exhaustive-deps to catch missed deps
testing_strategy: Render-count test: typing in filter, assert chart renders only when filter changes filtered array. Profiler test: React DevTools profiler before/after - assert chart 'Render reasons' shows only data-change. Identity test: re-render parent with same rawData and same filter - assert chart did not re-render.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would slap useMemo around everything indiscriminately - heavy memoization with stale deps causing bugs, or wrapping primitives whose memoization isn't worth the closure cost. The discipline is identifying which references actually need stability.

title: Build cross-tab broadcasting: state changes in one tab reflect immediately in all others
business_context: Power users keep multiple tabs of the app open. Saving in one tab doesn't update other tabs until refresh. Need cross-tab sync without server round-trips for state already known locally.
constraints: No server-side echo - tabs should sync directly
Must work even when tabs are in different windows
Logout in one tab must immediately log out all tabs
Cannot break Safari which has stricter BroadcastChannel rules

before_state.description: Each tab independent. Saves only visible in originating tab until reload.

issues: Other tabs see stale content until they reload

reasoning: BroadcastChannel API for tab-to-tab. Wrap in a small bus utility; fall back to storage events on Safari ITP. Channel topic per concern: 'auth' for logout, 'notes' for note updates. Each tab subscribes and updates local state on receive. Saves originate in one tab; before sending to server, post to channel optimistically (other tabs update immediately). Server still source of truth - sockets reconcile if there's drift.

after_state.description: BroadcastChannel-based cross-tab bus with storage-event fallback. Auth and entity-update channels.

files.purpose: Typed tab-bus with BroadcastChannel and storage-event fallback
Editor publishes update to channel; other tabs subscribe and reflect
Logout publishes to channel; all tabs redirect immediately

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add tab-bus utility, no consumers yet
Step 2: Wire auth channel into logout - test by opening two tabs and logging out
Step 3: Add notes channel; verify by editing in tab A and seeing tab B update
Step 4: Add other entity channels (cart, settings) as needed
testing_strategy: Cross-tab test in Playwright: open two contexts, post in one, assert receive in other. Safari ITP test: simulate via storage-event fallback. Logout cascade: 3 tabs open, logout in one, assert all redirect to login.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would rely solely on Socket.IO to echo - extra latency and unnecessary server load for state already in another tab. Or they'd use storage event everywhere - works but slower and noisier than BroadcastChannel where available.

title: Replace permissive JWT validation with strict claims checking and refresh-token rotation
business_context: Current JWT validation only checks signature. Tokens never rotate, never get revoked. Stolen tokens valid until expiry. Need short access tokens with refresh-token rotation, JTI tracking, and audience/issuer checks.
constraints: Cannot force all users to re-login
Refresh must happen seamlessly during long sessions
Revocation must take effect within 5 minutes
Cannot break mobile clients using bearer tokens

before_state.description: Single long-lived JWT, no refresh, no claims checking.

issues: 30d expiry - stolen token valid for a month
No JTI - cannot revoke specific tokens

reasoning: Standard rotation: short-lived access (15min) + long-lived refresh (30d). Refresh stored in HttpOnly cookie. Access in memory + Authorization header. On refresh: revoke old refresh, issue new pair, store refresh JTI in DB. Stolen-refresh detection: if a revoked refresh is presented, invalidate the whole family (chain). Claims: iss, aud, sub validated; JTI checked against revocation list. Tradeoff vs opaque tokens with introspection: more secure (revocable) but adds round-trip per request - significantly higher latency. Refresh+short-access JWTs is the standard.

after_state.description: Strict claim validation, refresh rotation with family-revoke on reuse, JTI tracking.

files.purpose: Issue/verify pairs with iss/aud/jti; rotate refresh and detect reuse via family revoke
Refresh endpoint rotates and re-issues; cookie path limited to refresh endpoint
Strict access check including revocation list lookup
Client refreshes on 401, single in-flight refresh promise prevents thunder

migration_strategy: Step 1: Issue new pair on next login; existing long-lived tokens accepted until expiry
Step 2: Ship refresh endpoint and cookie
Step 3: Update client to refresh on 401
Step 4: After grace period, reject tokens > 1h old, forcing migration to pairs
Step 5: Add revocation admin endpoint
testing_strategy: Refresh test: assert old refresh rejected after rotation; new pair works. Family revoke: replay an already-used refresh, assert subsequent refreshes in same family rejected. Race test: 10 parallel API calls during expired access, assert only one refresh call.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would issue new access tokens without invalidating old refresh - replay attack opens window. They'd skip family-revoke on reuse - attacker who got a stolen token can ride alongside legitimate user forever.

title: Replace prop-drilled theme/lang/featureFlag contexts with single CompositionRoot pattern
business_context: App has 8 root-level Providers nested; adding a new one is error-prone (wrong order breaks things). Want a single CompositionRoot that composes them and a unified useApp() hook for the common case.
constraints: Existing useTheme(), useLang(), useFlags() hooks must continue working
Provider order must be enforced by types
No global state
Test setup must remain ergonomic

before_state.description: Eight nested providers in App.tsx.

issues: Adding new provider is a merge conflict

reasoning: Define an array of providers and reduce; provider components are just (children) => JSX. CompositionRoot takes the array. The order is encoded in the array, single source of truth. For tests: TestProviders takes a subset. Tradeoff vs Provider compose libs (react-composer): adds dep. A 10-line reducer is enough.

after_state.description: Provider list + compose helper. Single CompositionRoot. Test helper accepts subset.

files.purpose: Reducer that nests providers in array order; outer first
Single source of truth for app providers and their order
App.tsx now one line - no chance to mis-order providers
Test harness composes a subset for ergonomic component tests

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add compose util and CompositionRoot alongside existing nested App
Step 2: Switch App to use CompositionRoot
Step 3: Migrate test files to TestProviders
Step 4: Remove old nested code
testing_strategy: Render test: mount CompositionRoot + a component that reads all 8 contexts, assert each value present. Order test: re-arrange APP_PROVIDERS array, assert behavior reflects new order (e.g., auth-dependent flags now works correctly).
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would use React.Children manipulation hacks that lose type safety. Or they'd skip the test helper, leading to duplicated provider stacks in every test file.

title: Build live 3D world map with country choropleth and tooltip linked to backend metrics
business_context: Customer success dashboard needs world view of active users per country with hover tooltip showing details. Currently a 2D table. Need interactive 3D globe with live updates.
constraints: Globe must remain interactive at 60fps with 1k labels
Country shapes loaded once and cached
Tooltips must render React content over WebGL
Disposes cleanly on route change

before_state.description: Table of countries.

reasoning: Use three-globe via @react-three/drei or vanilla globe.gl. Choropleth via per-country color. Hover detection via raycaster. Tooltip rendered as HTML overlay using <Html> from drei (rasterized but easy). Live updates via socket. Tradeoff vs deck.gl: powerful but heavy. globe.gl/three-globe is purpose-built and lighter.

after_state.description: Three.js globe with country choropleth, hover-overlay tooltip, socket-driven live metric updates.

files.purpose: Globe with choropleth, raycaster hover, HTML tooltip overlay, dispose on unmount
Geo namespace computes aggregations and broadcasts at 30s cadence

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add geo namespace and dependent country aggregation
Step 2: Ship globe behind feature flag for CS team
Step 3: Iterate on visual based on feedback
Step 4: Make default; keep table as alternate view
testing_strategy: FPS test: render 1k labels, assert >55fps. Disposal test: mount/unmount 50 times, assert no WebGL context leak. Hover test: Playwright moves mouse to known country center, assert tooltip text.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would re-create globe on every metrics update inside the effect deps - causes flicker and disposes prematurely. Or they'd use React for tooltip nested inside Canvas - r3f makes that work but most other setups need an HTML overlay.

title: Add tenant-aware feature flags with org-level overrides and per-user rollout
business_context: Marketing wants to enable new features to specific orgs first. Currently flags are global env vars. Need a flag service that supports global, org-override, and percentage rollout per user.
constraints: Flag evaluation must be < 1ms p99
Must work without external service in dev
Cannot trust client values - server is authoritative
Flags must be testable in unit tests

before_state.description: Global env-var flags.

issues: Cannot target specific orgs or users

reasoning: Flag rules: precedence is user-override > org-override > global default with rollout. Flags stored in collection FlagRule. Evaluator takes (flagName, ctx={userId, orgId, hashSeed}). Rollout uses deterministic hash(userId+flagName) mod 100 < percentage. Eval function pure - testable. Cache rules in memory with 30s TTL. Frontend gets flag values via /api/flags/me endpoint returning evaluated values for the current user, refreshed on org switch.

after_state.description: Rule-based flag service with org and user targeting; deterministic rollout hash; in-memory rule cache.

files.purpose: Pure evaluator with precedence and deterministic hash bucket; 30s rule cache
Returns evaluated flags for current user/org context
Server checks per-request, never trusts client
Client fetches evaluated flags; refetches on org switch

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add FlagRule model and service; seed with current env-var flags as global defaults
Step 2: Migrate one controller to use isEnabled; ship
Step 3: Migrate others; remove env-var flags
Step 4: Build admin UI for flag rules
Step 5: Document evaluation order for engineers
testing_strategy: Unit: assert precedence (user > org > rollout > default). Rollout test: with userId X, assert deterministic bucket. Cache invalidation test: change rule, call invalidateCache, assert new value used.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would put flag values in JWT - cannot change without re-login. They'd evaluate on client only - users can edit and bypass.

title: Build server-sent video room presence with media-state sync (mute/camera) for 50-person calls
business_context: Video calls have media state (audio muted, camera off, screen sharing) that other participants need to see. Currently each participant signals over WebRTC data channel - O(N^2) messages. Need server-mediated presence for media state with O(N) broadcast.
constraints: State changes must reach all peers within 200ms
Reconnects must restore peer's last known state
Server is source of truth for presence even though media is P2P
Cannot use existing chat sockets - separate concern

before_state.description: Each peer broadcasts mute state over its own WebRTC data channels.

issues: Late joiners don't know initial states

reasoning: Server keeps room presence map. Peers connect /call namespace, join room. On media-state change, peer emits to server, server broadcasts to room. Late joiners on connect receive full presence snapshot. Tradeoff vs continuing P2P: simpler server scales O(N) and handles late joiners.

after_state.description: Server-mediated presence with snapshot on join and broadcast on change.

files.purpose: Server-mediated presence with snapshot, joins, leaves, and media state changes
Hook subscribes to room presence + media changes
Renders peer list with live media state

migration_strategy: Step 1: Add /call namespace; clients continue using data channels alongside
Step 2: Migrate one client to use server-mediated presence; data channel becomes media-only
Step 3: All clients migrated
Step 4: Remove data-channel presence code
testing_strategy: Late joiner test: A joins room, mutes, B joins later, assert B sees A muted in snapshot. Disconnect test: A drops, assert peer-left fires. Scale test: 50 peers, each toggles mute, assert 50 broadcasts not 2500.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would continue P2P for presence - 50 peers means 50*49 = 2450 messages per state change. They'd skip the snapshot, leaving late joiners blind.

title: Extract embedded SDK into versioned package with backward-compat adapters
business_context: Customers embed our chat widget via a <script> tag. Widget code lives in the main app and ships whenever the app changes - causing breakages. Need to extract into a separate npm-published package with semver, while keeping existing embed snippets working.
constraints: Existing embed snippets must continue working without customer code change
Widget bundle must be < 50KB gzipped
Initialization must be backward compatible
Must support hot-swap during customer's session

before_state.description: Widget exposes window.MyChat - lives in main app bundle.

issues: Tied to main app bundle - any change in app risks widget regression
No versioning - customers have no idea what they're getting

reasoning: Split widget into separate package mychat-widget. Versioned publish. Loader script at chat.example.com/v1/loader.js detects best version (rolling 'v1' for latest 1.x). Bootstrap config can specify exact version: data-version attr. Old snippets work via the loader; new snippets pin version. Internal API: widget posts messages to customer page via window.postMessage instead of touching DOM directly - safer.

after_state.description: Extracted widget package, versioned loader, postMessage isolation.

files.purpose: Standalone widget class loaded as iframe; postMessage protocol with host
Lightweight loader that fetches versioned widget; legacy window.MyChat populated for old snippets

files.content:
<!-- Pre-extraction snippet still works via loader's window.MyChat shim -->
<script src="https://widget.example.com/loader.js"></script>
<script>
  MyChat.init({ apiKey: 'CUST_KEY_123' });
</script>
files.purpose: Documents that legacy snippet keeps working through compat shim

files.content:
<!-- Recommended snippet with version pinning -->
<script src="https://widget.example.com/v1/loader.js" data-version="1.4.2" data-api-key="CUST_KEY_123"></script>
files.purpose: New snippet with explicit version pin

migration_strategy: Step 1: Extract widget code to packages/widget; main app continues to bundle it
Step 2: Publish first version to npm + CDN
Step 3: Add loader.js with version detection
Step 4: Update docs - new customers use new snippet; existing customers untouched
Step 5: Remove widget code from main app after stable for 60 days
testing_strategy: Legacy snippet test: load loader.js, call window.MyChat.init, assert iframe appears. Version pin test: set data-version=1.3.0, assert that exact version loaded. Isolation test: customer page CSS does not affect widget styles (iframe protects).
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would extract but not provide the compat shim, breaking every customer's existing snippet. They'd inject widget styles directly into customer page, causing collisions. They'd publish without semver, breaking customers on patch bumps.

title: Lock down file download endpoint against path traversal and add streaming with range support
business_context: GET /api/files/:filename serves files from /var/uploads but does string concat allowing ../ traversal. Also loads entire file into memory before streaming. Need safe path resolution and streaming with HTTP range requests.
constraints: Filenames stored in DB are trusted, but :filename in URL must not be
Must support large video downloads with seeking (range requests)
Must not load > 64KB into memory at a time
Must keep existing API URL
Browser progressive download must work

before_state.description: String concat path; full read into memory.

issues: No range support - browser can't seek video

reasoning: Resolve and reject any path outside the uploads dir. Use fileId (UUID) routing instead of filename, with DB lookup for actual path. Stream via fs.createReadStream + parse Range header for partial content. Set Content-Type from stored metadata, not from filename guess. Tradeoff vs serving via S3: similar pattern but want local files supported. Same logic, different storage backend.

after_state.description: FileId routing + DB lookup, path resolution check, streaming with range support, proper headers.

files.purpose: Streams files with safe path resolution and HTTP range support
Routes by fileId not filename - DB controls actual path
Browser uses range requests automatically for seeking

migration_strategy: Step 1: Migrate URL from /files/:filename to /files/:fileId; redirect old URLs by lookup
Step 2: Ship streaming with range support; verify video seeking works
Step 3: Penetration retest for traversal
Step 4: Audit logs to confirm no traversal attempts hitting old paths
testing_strategy: Traversal test: GET /files/..%2F..%2Fetc%2Fpasswd, assert 400. Range test: send Range: bytes=0-99, assert 206 with first 100 bytes. Large file test: 1GB file, monitor process memory, assert < 100MB. Browser seek test: video element seeks to mid - assert range request fires.
what_breaks_naive_approach: A junior would add a regex to block ../ - bypassed by URL encoding. The structural fix is path.resolve and prefix check. They'd skip range support - video seeking re-downloads entire file each seek.
